					<div id="mw-content-text" class="mw-body-content"><div class="mw-subjectpageheader">
</div><div class="mw-content-ltr mw-parser-output" lang="en" dir="ltr"><div class="shortdescription nomobile noexcerpt noprint searchaux" style="display:none">Angel in Abrahamic religions</div>
<style data-mw-deduplicate="TemplateStyles:r1320445320">.mw-parser-output .hatnote{font-style:italic}.mw-parser-output div.hatnote{padding-left:1.6em;margin-bottom:0.5em}.mw-parser-output .hatnote i{font-style:normal}.mw-parser-output .hatnote+link+.hatnote{margin-top:-0.5em}@media print{body.ns-0 .mw-parser-output .hatnote{display:none!important}}</style><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">For other uses, see <a href="/wiki/Gabriel_(disambiguation)" class="mw-disambig" title="Gabriel (disambiguation)">Gabriel (disambiguation)</a>.</div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320"><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">"Angel Gabriel" redirects here. For the English passenger galleon, see <a href="/wiki/Angel_Gabriel_(ship)" title="Angel Gabriel (ship)">Angel Gabriel (ship)</a>. For the American comic book artist, see <a href="/wiki/Angel_Gabriele" title="Angel Gabriele">Angel Gabriele</a>.</div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320"><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">"Jibrail" redirects here. For the village in Iran, see <a href="/wiki/Jebreil" title="Jebreil">Jebreil</a>.</div>
<p class="mw-empty-elt">
</p>
<style data-mw-deduplicate="TemplateStyles:r1316064257">.mw-parser-output .infobox-subbox{padding:0;border:none;margin:-3px;width:auto;min-width:100%;font-size:100%;clear:none;float:none;background-color:transparent;color:inherit}.mw-parser-output .infobox-3cols-child{margin:-3px}.mw-parser-output .infobox .navbar{font-size:100%}@media screen{html.skin-theme-clientpref-night .mw-parser-output .infobox-full-data:not(.notheme)>div:not(.notheme)[style]{background:#1f1f23!important;color:#f8f9fa}}@media screen and (prefers-color-scheme:dark){html.skin-theme-clientpref-os .mw-parser-output .infobox-full-data:not(.notheme)>div:not(.notheme)[style]{background:#1f1f23!important;color:#f8f9fa}}@media(min-width:640px){body.skin--responsive .mw-parser-output .infobox-table{display:table!important}body.skin--responsive .mw-parser-output .infobox-table>caption{display:table-caption!important}body.skin--responsive .mw-parser-output .infobox-table>tbody{display:table-row-group}body.skin--responsive .mw-parser-output .infobox-table th,body.skin--responsive .mw-parser-output .infobox-table td{padding-left:inherit;padding-right:inherit}}</style><table class="infobox vcard"><tbody><tr><th colspan="2" class="infobox-above n" style="font-size:125%;background-color:gold; color:inherit;"><div class="honorific-prefix" style="display:inline;font-size: 77%; font-weight: normal;"><a href="/wiki/Saint" title="Saint">Saint</a></div><br><div style="display:inline;" class="fn">Gabriel</div><span style="display:none" data-plural="1"></span></th></tr><tr><td colspan="2" class="infobox-image"><span class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Frameless"><a href="/wiki/File:Leonardo_da_Vinci_Annunciazione_(dettaglio).jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/13/Leonardo_da_Vinci_Annunciazione_%28dettaglio%29.jpg/250px-Leonardo_da_Vinci_Annunciazione_%28dettaglio%29.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="167" style="--mw-file-upright: 1" class="mw-file-element mw-file-upright" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/13/Leonardo_da_Vinci_Annunciazione_%28dettaglio%29.jpg/500px-Leonardo_da_Vinci_Annunciazione_%28dettaglio%29.jpg 2x" data-file-width="5472" data-file-height="3648"></a></span><div class="infobox-caption">Detail of <i><a href="/wiki/Annunciation_(Leonardo_da_Vinci)" class="mw-redirect" title="Annunciation (Leonardo da Vinci)">Annunciation</a></i> by <a href="/wiki/Leonardo_da_Vinci" title="Leonardo da Vinci">Leonardo da Vinci</a>, c. 1472–1476</div></td></tr><tr><th colspan="2" class="infobox-header" style="background-color:gold; color:inherit;">Archangel<br> Divine Herald <br> Angel of Revelation</th></tr><tr><th scope="row" class="infobox-label">Venerated in</th><td class="infobox-data"><style data-mw-deduplicate="TemplateStyles:r1126788409">.mw-parser-output .plainlist ol,.mw-parser-output .plainlist ul{line-height:inherit;list-style:none;margin:0;padding:0}.mw-parser-output .plainlist ol li,.mw-parser-output .plainlist ul li{margin-bottom:0}</style><div class="plainlist">
<ul><li>All Christian denominations that <a href="/wiki/Veneration_of_saints" class="mw-redirect" title="Veneration of saints">venerate saints</a></li>
<li><a href="/wiki/Samaritanism" title="Samaritanism">Samaritanism</a></li>
<li><a href="/wiki/Judaism" title="Judaism">Judaism</a></li>
<li><a href="/wiki/Islam" title="Islam">Islam</a></li>
<li>and others<sup id="cite_ref-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-1"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>N 1<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup></li></ul>
</div></td></tr><tr><th scope="row" class="infobox-label"><a href="/wiki/Calendar_of_saints" title="Calendar of saints">Feast</a></th><td class="infobox-data"><link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1126788409"><div class="plainlist">
<ul><li>29 September with angels <a href="/wiki/Michael_(archangel)" title="Michael (archangel)">Michael</a> and <a href="/wiki/Raphael_(archangel)" title="Raphael (archangel)">Raphael</a> (<a href="/wiki/Catholic_Church" title="Catholic Church">Catholic Church</a>) (post-1969)</li>
<li>24 March (<a href="/wiki/Western_Rite_Orthodoxy" title="Western Rite Orthodoxy">Western Rite Orthodoxy</a> and <a href="/wiki/General_Roman_Calendar" title="General Roman Calendar">General Roman Calendar</a> before 1969)</li>
<li>26 March, 13 July (<a href="/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church" title="Eastern Orthodox Church">Eastern Orthodox Church</a>)</li>
<li>13 <a href="/wiki/Paoni" title="Paoni">Paoni</a>, 22 <a href="/wiki/Koiak" title="Koiak">Koiak</a> and 26 <a href="/wiki/Paoni" title="Paoni">Paoni</a> (Coptic Church)</li></ul>
</div>
<ul><li>28 December (Tahsas 19) and 26 July (Hamle 19) <a href="/wiki/Ethiopian_calendar" title="Ethiopian calendar">Ethiopian calendar</a></li></ul>
</td></tr><tr><th scope="row" class="infobox-label"><a href="/wiki/Saint_symbolism" title="Saint symbolism">Attributes</a></th><td class="infobox-data">White lily, trumpet, shining lantern, branch from Paradise, scroll and scepter<sup id="cite_ref-Ronner1993_2-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Ronner1993-2"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>1<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup></td></tr><tr><th scope="row" class="infobox-label"><a href="/wiki/Patron_saint" title="Patron saint">Patronage</a></th><td class="infobox-data">messengers (including telecommunication workers, postal workers, radio broadcasters, diplomats, and ambassadors), stamp collectors, <a href="/wiki/Santander,_Cebu" title="Santander, Cebu">Santander, Cebu</a><sup id="cite_ref-Guiley2004_3-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Guiley2004-3"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>2<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup></td></tr><tr><td colspan="2" class="infobox-full-data">Known as Cebrail (Djebraïl) in some cultures</td></tr></tbody></table>
<p>In <a href="/wiki/Judaism" title="Judaism">Judaism</a>, <a href="/wiki/Christianity" title="Christianity">Christianity</a>, <a href="/wiki/Islam" title="Islam">Islam</a>, and other <a href="/wiki/Abrahamic_religions" title="Abrahamic religions">Abrahamic religions</a>, <b>Gabriel</b> (<span class="rt-commentedText nowrap"><span class="IPA nopopups noexcerpt" lang="en-fonipa"><a href="/wiki/Help:IPA/English" title="Help:IPA/English">/<span style="border-bottom:1px dotted"><span title="/ˈ/: primary stress follows">ˈ</span><span title="/ɡ/: 'g' in 'guy'">ɡ</span><span title="/eɪ/: 'a' in 'face'">eɪ</span><span title="'b' in 'buy'">b</span><span title="'r' in 'rye'">r</span><span title="/i/: 'y' in 'happy'">i</span><span title="/ə/: 'a' in 'about'">ə</span><span title="'l' in 'lie'">l</span></span>/</a></span></span> <a href="/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key" title="Help:Pronunciation respelling key"><i title="English pronunciation respelling"><span style="font-size:90%">GAY</span>-bree-əl</i></a>)<sup id="cite_ref-4" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-4"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>N 2<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> or <b>Cebrail</b> (Djebraïl) in some cultures,<sup class="noprint Inline-Template Template-Fact" style="white-space:nowrap;">[<i><a href="/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed" title="Wikipedia:Citation needed"><span title="This claim needs references to reliable sources. (February 2026)">citation needed</span></a></i>]</sup> is an <a href="/wiki/Archangel" title="Archangel">archangel</a> with the power to announce <a href="/wiki/God" title="God">God</a>'s will to humankind as the messenger of God. He is mentioned in the <a href="/wiki/Hebrew_Bible" title="Hebrew Bible">Hebrew Bible</a>, the <a href="/wiki/New_Testament" title="New Testament">New Testament</a> and the <a href="/wiki/Quran" title="Quran">Quran</a>.
</p><p>In the <a href="/wiki/Book_of_Daniel" title="Book of Daniel">Book of Daniel</a>, Gabriel appears to the prophet <a href="/wiki/Daniel_(biblical_figure)" title="Daniel (biblical figure)">Daniel</a> to explain his visions.<sup id="cite_ref-5" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-5"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>3<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Gabriel also appears in the <a href="/wiki/Jewish_apocrypha" class="mw-redirect" title="Jewish apocrypha">Jewish apocryphal</a> <a href="/wiki/First_Book_of_Enoch" class="mw-redirect" title="First Book of Enoch">First Book of Enoch</a> (e.g., 1 Enoch 20:7–8) and other <a href="/wiki/Ancient_Hebrew_writings" title="Ancient Hebrew writings">ancient Hebrew writings</a> incompletely preserved or wholly lost in <a href="/wiki/Hebrew_language" title="Hebrew language">Hebrew</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-y573_6-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-y573-6"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>4<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Alongside the archangel <a href="/wiki/Michael_(archangel)" title="Michael (archangel)">Michael</a>, Gabriel is described as the <a href="/wiki/Guardian_angel" title="Guardian angel">guardian angel</a> of the <a href="/wiki/Israelites" title="Israelites">Israelites</a>, defending them against the angels of the other peoples.<sup id="cite_ref-7" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-7"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>5<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-8" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-8"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>6<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>In the New Testament's <a href="/wiki/Gospel_of_Luke" title="Gospel of Luke">Gospel of Luke</a>, Gabriel appears to <a href="/wiki/Zechariah,_father_of_John_the_Baptist" title="Zechariah, father of John the Baptist">Zechariah</a> foretelling the birth of <a href="/wiki/John_the_Baptist" title="John the Baptist">John the Baptist</a>. Gabriel later appears to <a href="/wiki/Mary,_mother_of_Jesus" title="Mary, mother of Jesus">Mary, mother of Jesus</a> to announce that she would conceive and bear a son (i.e., <a href="/wiki/Jesus" title="Jesus">Jesus</a>) via <a href="/wiki/Virgin_birth_of_Jesus" title="Virgin birth of Jesus">virgin birth</a>. Many <a href="/wiki/Branches_of_Christianity" class="mw-redirect" title="Branches of Christianity">branches of Christianity</a>—including <a href="/wiki/Eastern_Orthodoxy" title="Eastern Orthodoxy">Eastern Orthodoxy</a>, the <a href="/wiki/Catholic_Church" title="Catholic Church">Catholic Church</a>, <a href="/wiki/Lutheranism" title="Lutheranism">Lutheranism</a>, and <a href="/wiki/Anglicanism" title="Anglicanism">Anglicanism</a>—revere Gabriel as a <a href="/wiki/Saint" title="Saint">saint</a>.
</p><p>Islam regards Gabriel as an archangel sent by God to various prophets, including <a href="/wiki/Muhammad" title="Muhammad">Muhammad</a>. The first five verses of the <i><a href="/wiki/Al-Alaq" title="Al-Alaq">Al-Alaq</a></i>, the 96th <a href="/wiki/Chapter_of_the_Quran" class="mw-redirect" title="Chapter of the Quran">chapter of the Quran</a>, are believed by <a href="/wiki/Muslims" title="Muslims">Muslims</a> to have been the <a href="/w/index.php?title=Muhammad%E2%80%99s_first_revelation&action=edit&redlink=1" class="new" title="Muhammad’s first revelation (page does not exist)">first verses</a> of the <a href="/wiki/Wa%E1%B8%A5y" title="Waḥy">revelations</a> given by Gabriel to Muhammad. He is associated with communication about anything about God.
</p>
<meta property="mw:PageProp/toc">
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="Etymology">Etymology</h2><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Gabriel&action=edit&section=1" title="Edit section: Etymology"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<p>The name Gabriel (<a href="/wiki/Hebrew_language" title="Hebrew language">Hebrew:</a> גַּבְרִיאֵל, <i>Gaḇrīʾēl</i>) is composed of the first person singular possessive form of the Hebrew noun <i>gever</i> (גֶּבֶר), meaning "man",<sup id="cite_ref-BH1397_9-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-BH1397-9"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>7<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> and <i><a href="/wiki/El_(deity)" title="El (deity)">ʾĒl</a></i>, meaning "God" or "mighty one".<sup id="cite_ref-BH410_10-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-BH410-10"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>8<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> This would translate the archangel's name as "man of God". <a href="/wiki/Proclus_of_Constantinople" title="Proclus of Constantinople">Proclus of Constantinople</a>, in his Homily 1, stated that the meaning of Gabriel's name prefigured that Jesus, whose birth was <a href="/wiki/Annunciation" title="Annunciation">announced</a> by Gabriel, would be both man and God.<sup id="cite_ref-PPI2021_11-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-PPI2021-11"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>9<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>In his work, the <i>four homilies on the Missus Est</i>", <a href="/wiki/Bernard_of_Clairvaux" title="Bernard of Clairvaux">Saint Bernard</a> (1090–1153 AD) interpreted Gabriel's name as "the strength of God", and his symbolic function in the gospel story as announcement of the strength or virtue of Christ, both as the strength of God incarnate and as the strength given by God to the timorous people who would bring into the world a fearful and troublesome event. "Therefore it was an opportune choice that designated Gabriel for the work he had to accomplish, or rather, because he was to accomplish it therefore he was called Gabriel."<sup id="cite_ref-12" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-12"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>10<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="Judaism">Judaism</h2><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Gabriel&action=edit&section=2" title="Edit section: Judaism"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Hebrew_Bible">Hebrew Bible</h3><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Gabriel&action=edit&section=3" title="Edit section: Hebrew Bible"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:The_Archangel_Gabriel_appears_to_the_Prophet_Daniel.png" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b5/The_Archangel_Gabriel_appears_to_the_Prophet_Daniel.png/250px-The_Archangel_Gabriel_appears_to_the_Prophet_Daniel.png" decoding="async" width="250" height="268" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b5/The_Archangel_Gabriel_appears_to_the_Prophet_Daniel.png/500px-The_Archangel_Gabriel_appears_to_the_Prophet_Daniel.png 2x" data-file-width="635" data-file-height="680"></a><figcaption><i>The Archangel Gabriel appears to the Prophet Daniel</i>, attributed to <a href="/wiki/Francesco_Solimena" title="Francesco Solimena">Francesco Solimena</a> (1657–1747).</figcaption></figure>
<p>The only book in the <a href="/wiki/Hebrew_Bible" title="Hebrew Bible">Hebrew Bible</a> that explicitly mentions Gabriel is the <a href="/wiki/Book_of_Daniel" title="Book of Daniel">Book of Daniel</a>. Gabriel appears to the prophet <a href="/wiki/Daniel_(biblical_figure)" title="Daniel (biblical figure)">Daniel</a> to explain his visions (<a href="/wiki/Daniel_8" title="Daniel 8">Daniel 8</a>:15–26, <a href="/wiki/Prophecy_of_Seventy_Weeks" title="Prophecy of Seventy Weeks">9</a>:21–27). Later, in <a href="/wiki/Daniel%27s_final_vision" title="Daniel's final vision">Daniel's final vision</a>, an angel, not named but likely Gabriel again, appears to him and speaks of receiving help from <a href="/wiki/Michael_(archangel)" title="Michael (archangel)">Michael</a> in battle against the prince of Persia and also Michael's role in times to come. The Book of Daniel contains the first instances of named angels in the Hebrew Bible. Gabriel's main function in the Book of Daniel is that of revealer, responsible for interpreting Daniel's visions, a role he continues to have in later traditions. In Daniel 10–12, while Gabriel is not named directly, many scholars infer his continued presence as the messenger who delivers Daniel’s final apocalyptic revelations.
</p><p>Though he is not specifically named, the "man clothed with linen" mentioned in chapters <a href="/wiki/Ezekiel_9" title="Ezekiel 9">9</a> and <a href="/wiki/Ezekiel_10" title="Ezekiel 10">10</a> of the <a href="/wiki/Book_of_Ezekiel" title="Book of Ezekiel">Book of Ezekiel</a> is interpreted as Gabriel in <a href="/wiki/Yoma" title="Yoma">Yoma</a> 77a of the <a href="/wiki/Talmud" title="Talmud">Babylonian Talmud</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-JE1912_13-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-JE1912-13"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>11<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Intertestamental_literature">Intertestamental literature</h3><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Gabriel&action=edit&section=4" title="Edit section: Intertestamental literature"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<p>Gabriel is not referred to as an archangel in the Hebrew Bible or the New Testament. However, a wealth of <a href="/wiki/Non-canonical_books_referenced_in_the_Bible" title="Non-canonical books referenced in the Bible">Jewish literature</a> was written during the <a href="/wiki/Second_Temple_period" title="Second Temple period">Second Temple period</a> (516 BC–70 AD). Much of the literature produced during this <a href="/wiki/Intertestamental_period" title="Intertestamental period">intertestamental period</a> was of the <a href="/wiki/Apocalyptic_literature" title="Apocalyptic literature">apocalyptic</a> <a href="/wiki/Literary_genre" title="Literary genre">genre</a>. The <a href="/wiki/List_of_angels_in_theology" title="List of angels in theology">names and ranks of angels</a> and <a href="/wiki/List_of_theological_demons" title="List of theological demons">demons</a> were greatly expanded in this literature, and each had particular duties and status before God. Gabriel was first referred to as an archangel in these texts.
</p><p>In particular, there are many references to Gabriel in the <a href="/wiki/Book_of_Enoch" title="Book of Enoch">Book of Enoch</a>. According to the book, Michael, <a href="/wiki/Uriel" title="Uriel">Uriel</a>, <a href="/wiki/Raphael_(archangel)" title="Raphael (archangel)">Raphael</a>, and Gabriel complain to God about the many wrongs perpetrated by <a href="/wiki/Azazel" title="Azazel">Azazel</a> and <a href="/wiki/Samyaza" title="Samyaza">Samyaza</a> (especially the fact that they revealed "eternal secrets" and sins to mankind and defiled themselves with women who later gave birth to <a href="/wiki/Nephilim" title="Nephilim">giant offspring</a>).<sup id="cite_ref-FOOTNOTECharles1913192–193_14-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTECharles1913192–193-14"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>12<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> As a result, God decides to destroy the Earth (which has been corrupted by the <a href="/wiki/Fallen_angel" title="Fallen angel">fallen angels</a>, led by Azazel and Samyaza) and all of its inhabitants except for <a href="/wiki/Noah" title="Noah">Noah</a>. He sends Gabriel and the other archangels to go after the fallen angels and cast them into the darkness until <a href="/wiki/Last_Judgment" title="Last Judgment">the day of their judgment</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-FOOTNOTECharles1913193–195_15-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTECharles1913193–195-15"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>13<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> In Chapter 20, Gabriel is listed as one of seven holy angels (Uriel, Raphael, <a href="/wiki/Raguel_(angel)" title="Raguel (angel)">Raguel</a>, Michael, <a href="/wiki/Sariel" title="Sariel">Saraqâêl</a>, Gabriel, and <a href="/wiki/Jerahmeel_(archangel)" title="Jerahmeel (archangel)">Remiel</a>) who <a href="/wiki/Watcher_(angel)" title="Watcher (angel)">watch</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-FOOTNOTECharles1913201_16-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTECharles1913201-16"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>14<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> In Chapter 40, Gabriel is listed as one of four presences (Michael, Raphael, Gabriel, and <a href="/wiki/Phanuel_(angel)" title="Phanuel (angel)">Phanuel</a>) who stand on the four sides of God.<sup id="cite_ref-FOOTNOTECharles1913211–212_17-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTECharles1913211–212-17"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>15<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> These four archangels will be the ones to cast the fallen angels into the abyss of condemnation on Judgment Day.<sup id="cite_ref-FOOTNOTECharles1913220–221_18-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTECharles1913220–221-18"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>16<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> The final reference to Gabriel in the Book of Enoch is found in Chapter 71: "And that Head of Days came with Michael and Gabriel, Raphael and Phanuel, thousands and ten thousands of angels without number."<sup id="cite_ref-FOOTNOTECharles1913237_19-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTECharles1913237-19"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>17<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>The Book of Enoch is not considered to be <a href="/wiki/Biblical_canon" title="Biblical canon">canonical</a> scripture by most Jewish or Christian church bodies, although it is part of the biblical canon used by the <a href="/wiki/Beta_Israel" title="Beta Israel">Ethiopian Jewish community</a>, as well as the <a href="/wiki/Ethiopian_Orthodox_Tewahedo_Church" title="Ethiopian Orthodox Tewahedo Church">Ethiopian</a> and <a href="/wiki/Eritrean_Orthodox_Tewahedo_Church" title="Eritrean Orthodox Tewahedo Church">Eritrean Orthodox Tewahedo Churches</a>.
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Rabbinic_Judaism">Rabbinic Judaism</h3><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Gabriel&action=edit&section=5" title="Edit section: Rabbinic Judaism"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<p>According to <a href="/wiki/Rabbinic_Judaism" title="Rabbinic Judaism">Rabbinic Judaism</a>, Gabriel — along with Michael, Uriel, and Raphael — is one of the four angels that stand at the four sides of God’s throne and serve as <a href="/wiki/Guardian_angel" title="Guardian angel">guardian angels</a> of the four parts of the Earth. Michael stands at the right hand of God, while Gabriel (who ranks beneath Michael) stands at the left. Michael and Gabriel often work together, but Michael is mainly occupied in heaven, while Gabriel (as the messenger of God) typically executes God’s will on earth. Like all the angels, Gabriel has wings, but otherwise takes the form of a man. Gabriel is also associated with the metal gold (the color of fire).<sup id="cite_ref-JE1912_13-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-JE1912-13"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>11<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p><a href="/wiki/Shimon_ben_Lakish" title="Shimon ben Lakish">Shimon ben Lakish</a> (an <i><a href="/wiki/Amoraim" title="Amoraim">amora</a></i> of the third century) concluded that the angelic names of Michael, Raphael, and Gabriel came out of the Babylonian exile (Gen. Rab. 48:9). Alongside the archangel Michael, Gabriel is described as the guardian angel of Israel, defending the Israelites against the angels of the other nations.<sup id="cite_ref-Everson_20-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Everson-20"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>18<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Mystical_Judaism">Mystical Judaism</h3><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Gabriel&action=edit&section=6" title="Edit section: Mystical Judaism"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<p>Gabriel is one of God's archangels in the <a href="/wiki/Primary_texts_of_Kabbalah" title="Primary texts of Kabbalah">Kabbalah literature</a>. He is portrayed as working in concert with Michael as part of God's court, and he is identified with the <a href="/wiki/Counting_of_the_Omer" title="Counting of the Omer">sefira</a> of <a href="/wiki/Yesod" title="Yesod">Yesod</a>. Gabriel is not to be prayed to because only God can answer prayers and sends Gabriel as his agent.<sup id="cite_ref-JE1912_13-2" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-JE1912-13"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>11<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>According to <a href="/wiki/Jewish_mythology" title="Jewish mythology">Jewish mythology</a>, in the <a href="/wiki/Garden_of_Eden" title="Garden of Eden">Garden of Eden</a> there is a <a href="/wiki/Tree_of_life_(biblical)" title="Tree of life (biblical)">tree of life</a> or the "tree of souls"<sup id="cite_ref-Scholem1990_21-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Scholem1990-21"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>19<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> that blossoms and produces new souls, which fall into the <a href="/wiki/Guf" title="Guf">Guf</a>, the treasury of souls. Gabriel reaches into the treasury and takes out the first soul that comes into his hand.
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="Christianity">Christianity</h2><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Gabriel&action=edit&section=7" title="Edit section: Christianity"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="New_Testament">New Testament</h3><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Gabriel&action=edit&section=8" title="Edit section: New Testament"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Alexandr_Ivanov_010.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/13/Alexandr_Ivanov_010.jpg/250px-Alexandr_Ivanov_010.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="167" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/13/Alexandr_Ivanov_010.jpg/500px-Alexandr_Ivanov_010.jpg 2x" data-file-width="603" data-file-height="403"></a><figcaption>Gabriel announcing the birth of <a href="/wiki/John_the_Baptist" title="John the Baptist">John the Baptist</a> to <a href="/wiki/Zechariah,_father_of_John_the_Baptist" title="Zechariah, father of John the Baptist">Zechariah</a>, by <a href="/wiki/Alexander_Andreyevich_Ivanov" title="Alexander Andreyevich Ivanov">Alexander Andreyevich Ivanov</a>, 1824</figcaption></figure>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:ANGELICO,_Fra_Annunciation,_1437-46_(2236990916).jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1b/ANGELICO%2C_Fra_Annunciation%2C_1437-46_%282236990916%29.jpg/250px-ANGELICO%2C_Fra_Annunciation%2C_1437-46_%282236990916%29.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="174" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1b/ANGELICO%2C_Fra_Annunciation%2C_1437-46_%282236990916%29.jpg/500px-ANGELICO%2C_Fra_Annunciation%2C_1437-46_%282236990916%29.jpg 2x" data-file-width="1008" data-file-height="700"></a><figcaption>Gabriel announcing the <a href="/wiki/Incarnation_(Christianity)" title="Incarnation (Christianity)">incarnation</a> to <a href="/wiki/Mary,_mother_of_Jesus" title="Mary, mother of Jesus">Mary</a>, <a href="/wiki/Annunciation_(Fra_Angelico,_San_Marco)" title="Annunciation (Fra Angelico, San Marco)">by Fra Angelico</a>, c. 1440–1445</figcaption></figure>
<p>Gabriel's first appearance in the <a href="/wiki/New_Testament" title="New Testament">New Testament</a> is found in <a href="/wiki/Luke_1#John_the_Baptist's_parents_(1:5–25)" title="Luke 1">the first part</a> of <a href="/wiki/Luke_1" title="Luke 1">Chapter 1</a> of the <a href="/wiki/Gospel_of_Luke" title="Gospel of Luke">Gospel of Luke</a>, in which he relates the annunciation of the birth of <a href="/wiki/John_the_Baptist" title="John the Baptist">John the Baptist</a>. John's father <a href="/wiki/Zechariah,_father_of_John_the_Baptist" title="Zechariah, father of John the Baptist">Zechariah</a> was childless because his wife <a href="/wiki/Elizabeth,_mother_of_John_the_Baptist" title="Elizabeth, mother of John the Baptist">Elizabeth</a> was barren. An angel appears to Zechariah to announce the birth of his son. When Zechariah questions the angel, the angel identifies himself as Gabriel.(<a href="https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Bible_(King_James)/Luke#1:5" class="extiw" title="s:Bible (King James)/Luke">Luke 1:5–25</a>)
</p><p>Gabriel appears again in <a href="/wiki/Luke_1#The_annunciation_(1:26–38)" title="Luke 1">the second part</a> of Chapter 1 of the Gospel of Luke, this time to <a href="/wiki/Annunciation" title="Annunciation">announce the birth of Jesus</a> to <a href="/wiki/Mary,_mother_of_Jesus" title="Mary, mother of Jesus">Mary</a>.(<a href="https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Bible_(King_James)/Luke#1:26" class="extiw" title="s:Bible (King James)/Luke">Luke 1:26–38</a>) While in the first passage the angel identifies himself as Gabriel, in the second passage it is the author of Luke who identifies the angel as Gabriel.
</p><p>The only other named angels in the New Testament are Michael (in <a href="https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Bible_(King_James)/Jude#1:9" class="extiw" title="s:Bible (King James)/Jude">Jude 1:9</a> and <a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Revelation+12:7&version=7">Revelation 12:7</a>) and <a href="/wiki/Abaddon#Christianity" title="Abaddon">Abaddon</a> (in <a href="https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Bible_(King_James)/Revelation#9:11" class="extiw" title="s:Bible (King James)/Revelation">Revelation 9:11</a>).
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Non-canonical_texts">Non-canonical texts</h3><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Gabriel&action=edit&section=9" title="Edit section: Non-canonical texts"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<p>Gabriel is more frequently referenced in early Christian <a href="/wiki/Pseudepigrapha" title="Pseudepigrapha">pseudepigraphic texts</a> than in any of the <a href="/wiki/Biblical_canon" title="Biblical canon">canonical</a> Biblical texts. For example, Gabriel is mentioned in some of the <a href="/wiki/Infancy_gospels" title="Infancy gospels">infancy gospels</a> (e.g., Chapter 7 of the <a href="/wiki/Libellus_de_Nativitate_Sanctae_Mariae" title="Libellus de Nativitate Sanctae Mariae">Nativity Gospel of Mary</a>,<sup id="cite_ref-FOOTNOTEHone188022_22-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHone188022-22"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>20<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Chapter 9 of the <a href="/wiki/Gospel_of_James" title="Gospel of James">Protevangelium of James</a>,<sup id="cite_ref-FOOTNOTEHone188030_23-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHone188030-23"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>21<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> and Chapter 1 of the <a href="/wiki/Arabic_Infancy_Gospel" title="Arabic Infancy Gospel">First Gospel of the Infancy of Jesus Christ</a><sup id="cite_ref-FOOTNOTEHone188038_24-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHone188038-24"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>22<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>). Gabriel is also mentioned in some of the early Christian <a href="/wiki/Apocalypse" title="Apocalypse">apocalyptic</a> texts, such as the <a href="/wiki/Greek_Apocalypse_of_Ezra" title="Greek Apocalypse of Ezra">Greek Apocalypse of Ezra</a><sup id="cite_ref-BHEsdras_25-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-BHEsdras-25"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>23<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> and the <a href="/wiki/2_Enoch" title="2 Enoch">Second Book of Enoch</a> (e.g., Chapter 21<sup id="cite_ref-FOOTNOTECharles189626,_27_26-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTECharles189626,_27-26"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>24<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> and Chapter 24<sup id="cite_ref-FOOTNOTECharles189631_27-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTECharles189631-27"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>25<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>).
</p><p>In <a href="/wiki/Gnosticism" title="Gnosticism">Gnosticism</a>, angels are portrayed as belonging to a pantheon of spiritual beings involved in the creation of the world. According to one ancient <a href="/wiki/List_of_Gnostic_texts" title="List of Gnostic texts">Gnostic manuscript</a>, the <a href="/wiki/Holy_Book_of_the_Great_Invisible_Spirit" title="Holy Book of the Great Invisible Spirit">Holy Book of the Great Invisible Spirit</a>, Gabriel is a divine being and inhabitant of the <a href="/wiki/Pleroma#Gnosticism" title="Pleroma">pleroma</a> that existed before the <a href="/wiki/Demiurge#Gnosticism" title="Demiurge">demiurge</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-Robinson2007_28-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Robinson2007-28"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>26<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> There is also a reference to Gabriel in Chapter 17 of the <a href="/wiki/Gospel_of_Judas" title="Gospel of Judas">Gospel of Judas</a>, a Gnostic text dated to 280 AD.<sup id="cite_ref-Kasser2006_29-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Kasser2006-29"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>27<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Latter-day_Saints">Latter-day Saints</h3><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Gabriel&action=edit&section=10" title="Edit section: Latter-day Saints"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<p>In the theology of <a href="/wiki/The_Church_of_Jesus_Christ_of_Latter-day_Saints" title="The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints">the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints</a>, Gabriel is believed to have lived a mortal life as the prophet <a href="/wiki/Noah" title="Noah">Noah</a>. The two are regarded as the same individual; Noah being his mortal name and Gabriel being his heavenly name.<sup id="cite_ref-Skinner1992_30-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Skinner1992-30"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>28<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-:2_31-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-:2-31"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>29<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Feast_day">Feast day</h3><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Gabriel&action=edit&section=11" title="Edit section: Feast day"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<p>The feast day of Saint Gabriel the Archangel was exclusively celebrated on 18 March according to many sources dating between 1588 and 1921; unusually, a source published in 1856<sup id="cite_ref-32" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-32"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>30<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> has the feast celebrated on 7 April for unknown reasons (a parenthetical note states that the day is normally celebrated on 18 March). Writer <a href="/wiki/Elizabeth_Drayson" title="Elizabeth Drayson">Elizabeth Drayson</a> mentions the feast being celebrated on 18 March 1588 in her 2013 book "The Lead Books of Granada".<sup id="cite_ref-Drayson2016_33-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Drayson2016-33"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>31<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>One of the oldest out-of-print sources placing the feast on 18 March, first published in 1608, is <i>Flos sanctorum: historia general de la vida y hechos de Jesu-Christo ... y de los santos de que reza y haze fiesta la Iglesia Catholica ...</i> by the Spanish writer <a href="/wiki/Alonso_de_Villegas" title="Alonso de Villegas">Alonso de Villegas</a>; a newer edition of this book was published in 1794.<sup id="cite_ref-Villegas1794_34-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Villegas1794-34"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>32<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Another source published in Ireland in 1886 the <i><a href="/wiki/Irish_Ecclesiastical_Record" title="Irish Ecclesiastical Record">Irish Ecclesiastical Record</a></i> also mentions 18 March.<sup id="cite_ref-Irish1886_35-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Irish1886-35"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>33<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>The Feast of Saint Gabriel was included by <a href="/wiki/Pope_Benedict_XV" title="Pope Benedict XV">Pope Benedict XV</a> in the <a href="/wiki/General_Roman_Calendar" title="General Roman Calendar">General Roman Calendar</a> in 1921, for celebration on 24 March.<sup id="cite_ref-36" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-36"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>34<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> In 1969, the day was officially transferred to 29 September for celebration in conjunction with the feast of the archangels Ss. Michael and Raphael.<sup id="cite_ref-37" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-37"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>35<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Today, the 29 September date (known as <a href="/wiki/Michaelmas" title="Michaelmas">Michaelmas</a>) has been adopted by not only the Catholic Church, but also the <a href="/wiki/Church_of_England" title="Church of England">Church of England</a>, the <a href="/wiki/Lutheranism" title="Lutheranism">Lutheran churches</a>, the <a href="/wiki/Anglican_Communion" title="Anglican Communion">Anglican Communion</a>, and the <a href="/wiki/Western_Rite_Orthodoxy" title="Western Rite Orthodoxy">Western Orthodox churches</a>.
</p><p>The <a href="/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church" title="Eastern Orthodox Church">Eastern Orthodox Church</a> and those <a href="/wiki/Eastern_Catholic_Churches" title="Eastern Catholic Churches">Eastern Catholic Churches</a> that follow the <a href="/wiki/Byzantine_Rite" title="Byzantine Rite">Byzantine Rite</a> celebrate the <a href="/wiki/Michaelmas" title="Michaelmas">Feast of the Archangels</a> (Synaxis of the Archangel Michael and the Other Bodiless Powers) on 8 November. For those churches that follow the traditional <a href="/wiki/Julian_Calendar" class="mw-redirect" title="Julian Calendar">Julian Calendar</a>, 8 November currently falls on 21 November of the modern <a href="/wiki/Gregorian_Calendar" class="mw-redirect" title="Gregorian Calendar">Gregorian Calendar</a>, a difference of 13 days. Eastern Orthodox commemorate Gabriel not only at the Feast of the Archangels, but also on two other days:
</p>
<ul><li>26 March, the "<a href="/wiki/Synaxis" title="Synaxis">Synaxis</a> of the Archangel Gabriel" and celebrates his role in the <a href="/wiki/Annunciation" title="Annunciation">Annunciation</a></li>
<li>13 July, also known as the "Synaxis of the Archangel Gabriel", which celebrates all the appearances and miracles attributed to Gabriel throughout history. The feast was first established on <a href="/wiki/Mount_Athos" title="Mount Athos">Mount Athos</a> when, in the 9th century, during the reign of Emperor <a href="/wiki/Basil_II" title="Basil II">Basil II</a> and Empress Constantina Porphyrogenitus and while <a href="/wiki/Nicholas_II_Chrysoberges" class="mw-redirect" title="Nicholas II Chrysoberges">Nicholas Chrysoverges</a> was <a href="/wiki/Ecumenical_Patriarch_of_Constantinople" title="Ecumenical Patriarch of Constantinople">Patriarch of Constantinople</a>, Gabriel appeared in a cell<sup id="cite_ref-38" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-38"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>36<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> near <a href="/wiki/Karyes" title="Karyes">Karyes</a>, where he wrote with his finger on a stone tablet the hymn to the <a href="/wiki/Theotokos" title="Theotokos">Theotokos</a>, "<a href="/wiki/Axion_Estin" title="Axion Estin">It is truly meet ...</a>".<sup id="cite_ref-Velimirovic1985_39-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Velimirovic1985-39"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>37<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup></li></ul>
<p>Saint Gabriel the Archangel is <a href="/wiki/Calendar_of_saints" title="Calendar of saints">commemorated</a> on the <a href="/wiki/Vigil_(liturgy)" title="Vigil (liturgy)">vigil</a> of the <a href="/wiki/Feast_of_the_Annunciation" title="Feast of the Annunciation">Feast of the Annunciation</a> by <a href="/wiki/Antiochian_Western_Rite_Vicariate" title="Antiochian Western Rite Vicariate">Antiochian Western Rite Vicariate</a><sup id="cite_ref-40" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-40"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>38<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> and <a href="/wiki/Russian_Orthodox_Church_Outside_of_Russia#Western_Rite_in_the_ROCOR" title="Russian Orthodox Church Outside of Russia">Western Rite in the ROCOR</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-41" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-41"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>39<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>The <a href="/wiki/Coptic_Orthodox_Church" title="Coptic Orthodox Church">Coptic Orthodox Church</a> celebrates Gabriel's feast on 13 <a href="/wiki/Paoni" title="Paoni">Paoni</a>,<sup id="cite_ref-42" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-42"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>40<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> 22 <a href="/wiki/Koiak" title="Koiak">Koiak</a>, and 26 Paoni.<sup id="cite_ref-43" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-43"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>41<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>  One medieval Coptic work, the <i><a href="/wiki/Investiture_of_the_Archangel_Gabriel" title="Investiture of the Archangel Gabriel">Investiture of the Archangel Gabriel</a></i>, attributes the feast day of 22 Koiak to the day Gabriel was given the rank of archangel in heaven.<sup id="cite_ref-Jenott2020_44-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Jenott2020-44"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>42<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>The <a href="/wiki/Ethiopian_Church" class="mw-redirect" title="Ethiopian Church">Ethiopian Church</a> celebrates Gabriel's feast on 18 December (in the Ethiopian calendar), with a sizeable number of its believers making a pilgrimage to a church dedicated to "Saint Gabriel" in <a href="/wiki/Kulubi" title="Kulubi">Kulubi</a> and Wonkshet on that day.<sup id="cite_ref-45" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-45"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>43<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Gabriel's_horn"><span id="Gabriel.27s_horn"></span>Gabriel's horn</h3><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Gabriel&action=edit&section=12" title="Edit section: Gabriel's horn"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<p>A familiar <a href="/wiki/Literary_trope" class="mw-redirect" title="Literary trope">literary trope</a> of Gabriel has him blowing a <a href="/wiki/Trumpet" title="Trumpet">trumpet</a> blast to announce the resurrection of the dead at the end of time. However, though the Bible mentions a trumpet blast preceding the resurrection of the dead, it never specifies Gabriel as the trumpeter. Different passages state different things: the angels of the Son of Man (<a href="/wiki/Matthew_24" title="Matthew 24">Matthew 24</a>:31); the voice of the Son of God (<a href="/wiki/John_5" title="John 5">John 5</a>:25–29); God's trumpet (<a href="/wiki/First_Epistle_to_the_Thessalonians" title="First Epistle to the Thessalonians">I Thessalonians</a> 4:16); seven angels sounding a series of blasts (<a href="/wiki/Revelation_8" title="Revelation 8">Revelation 8</a>–<a href="/wiki/Revelation_11" title="Revelation 11">11</a>); or simply "a trumpet will sound" (<a href="/wiki/First_Epistle_to_the_Corinthians" title="First Epistle to the Corinthians">I Corinthians</a> 15:52).<sup id="cite_ref-svm_46-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-svm-46"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>44<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Likewise the early Christian <a href="/wiki/Church_Fathers" title="Church Fathers">Church Fathers</a> do not mention Gabriel as a trumpeter; and in Jewish and Muslim traditions, Gabriel is again not identified as a trumpeter.<sup id="cite_ref-47" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-47"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>45<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>The earliest known identification of Gabriel as a trumpeter comes from the "Hymn for Protection in the Night", attributed to the Armenian Saint <a href="/wiki/Nerses_IV_the_Gracious" title="Nerses IV the Gracious">Nerses IV the Gracious</a> (1102 – 1173):<sup id="cite_ref-orthodoxchristianity_48-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-orthodoxchristianity-48"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>46<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
<blockquote><p>The sound of Gabriel's trumpet on the last night, make us worthy to hear, and to stand on your right hand among the sheep with lanterns of inextinguishable light; to be like the five wise virgins, so that with the bridegroom in the bride chamber we, his spiritual brides may enter into glory.</p></blockquote>
<p>A 1455 Armenian manuscript shows Gabriel sounding his trumpet as the dead climb out of their graves.<sup id="cite_ref-49" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-49"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>47<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Another example occurs in <a href="/wiki/John_Milton" title="John Milton">John Milton</a>'s <i><a href="/wiki/Paradise_Lost" title="Paradise Lost">Paradise Lost</a></i> (1667):<sup id="cite_ref-svm_46-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-svm-46"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>44<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-50" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-50"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>48<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
<blockquote><div class="poem">
<p>Betwixt these rockie pillars Gabriel sat<br>
Chief of the Angelic guards (IV.545f) ...<br>
He ended, and the Son gave signal high<br>
To the bright minister that watch'd, he blew<br>
His trumpet, heard in Oreb since perhaps<br>
When God descended, and perhaps once more<br>
To sound at general doom. (XI.72ff).
</p>
</div></blockquote>
<p>It is unclear whether Milton was inspired by the Armenian works, though they presumably have a common source.<sup id="cite_ref-svm_46-2" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-svm-46"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>44<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>The image of Gabriel's trumpet blast to announce the end of time was taken up in <a href="/wiki/Evangelicalism" title="Evangelicalism">evangelical Christianity</a>, where it became widespread, notably in <a href="/wiki/Spirituals" title="Spirituals">African American spirituals</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-51" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-51"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>49<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="Islam"><span class="anchor" id="Gabriel_in_Islam"></span><span class="anchor" id="Jibril"></span>Islam</h2><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Gabriel&action=edit&section=13" title="Edit section: Islam"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320"><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">See also: <a href="/wiki/R%C5%AB%E1%B8%A5" title="Rūḥ">Rūḥ</a></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320"><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">"Gibril" and "Jibril" redirect here. For other uses, see <a href="/wiki/Gibril_(disambiguation)" class="mw-disambig" title="Gibril (disambiguation)">Gibril (disambiguation)</a> and <a href="/wiki/Jibril_(disambiguation)" class="mw-disambig" title="Jibril (disambiguation)">Jibril (disambiguation)</a>.</div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Miniatura_Maometto.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/76/Miniatura_Maometto.jpg/250px-Miniatura_Maometto.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="288" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/7/76/Miniatura_Maometto.jpg 2x" data-file-width="487" data-file-height="561"></a><figcaption>A 16th-century Siyer-i Nebi image of the archangel Jibril (Gabriel) visiting Muhammad</figcaption></figure>
<p>Gabriel (<a href="/wiki/Hejaz" title="Hejaz">Hejazis</a> <a href="/wiki/Arabic_language" class="mw-redirect" title="Arabic language">Arabic</a>: <span lang="ar" dir="rtl">جِبْرِيل</span>, <small><a href="/wiki/Romanization_of_Arabic" title="Romanization of Arabic">romanized</a>: </small><span title="Arabic-language romanization"><i lang="ar-Latn">Jibrīl</i></span>;<sup id="cite_ref-52" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-52"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>50<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> also <a href="/wiki/Arabic_language" class="mw-redirect" title="Arabic language">Arabic</a>: <span lang="ar" dir="rtl">جبرائيل</span>, <small><a href="/wiki/Romanization_of_Arabic" title="Romanization of Arabic">romanized</a>: </small><span title="Arabic-language romanization"><i lang="ar-Latn">Jibrāʾīl</i></span>; other canonical writings include: <i>Jabrāʾīl</i>, ''<i>Jabrīl</i>, <i>Jabrāyīl</i>, and <i>Jibrāʾīn</i><sup id="cite_ref-53" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-53"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>51<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>) derived from the <a href="/wiki/Hebrew_language" title="Hebrew language">Hebrew</a>: <span lang="he" dir="rtl">גַּבְרִיאֵל</span>, <small><a href="/wiki/Romanization_of_Hebrew" title="Romanization of Hebrew">romanized</a>: </small><span title="Hebrew-language romanization"><i lang="he-Latn">Gaḇrīʾēl</i></span>)<sup id="cite_ref-EoQ_54-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-EoQ-54"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>52<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-Reynolds2014_55-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Reynolds2014-55"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>53<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-EI2_56-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-EI2-56"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>54<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-57" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-57"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>55<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> in many places in the Qur'an, is revered as one of the primary archangels and as the Angel of Revelation in Islam.<sup id="cite_ref-EoQ_54-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-EoQ-54"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>52<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-Reynolds2014_55-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Reynolds2014-55"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>53<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-EI2_56-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-EI2-56"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>54<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> He is primarily mentioned in the verses <span class="plainlinks"><a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://quran.com/2?startingVerse=97">2:97</a></span>, <span class="plainlinks"><a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://quran.com/2?startingVerse=98">2:98</a></span> and <span class="plainlinks"><a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://quran.com/66?startingVerse=4">66:4</a></span> of the Quran. However, the Quranic text doesn't refer to him as an angel.<sup id="cite_ref-Reynolds2014_55-2" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Reynolds2014-55"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>53<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> In the Quran, the archangel Gabriel appears named in <span class="plainlinks"><a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://quran.com/2?startingVerse=97">2:97</a></span> and <span class="plainlinks"><a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://quran.com/66?startingVerse=4">66:4</a></span>, as well as in <span class="plainlinks"><a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://quran.com/2?startingVerse=98">2:98</a></span>, where he is mentioned along with the archangel Michael.<sup id="cite_ref-EoQ_54-2" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-EoQ-54"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>52<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p><a href="/wiki/Tafsir" title="Tafsir">Tafsir</a> (Exegetical Quranic literature) narrates that Muhammad saw the archangel Gabriel in his full angelic splendor only twice, the first time being when he received his first revelation.<sup id="cite_ref-EI2_56-2" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-EI2-56"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>54<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Islamic tradition holds that Gabriel was sent to numerous pre-Islamic Biblical prophets with revelation and divine injunctions, including <a href="/wiki/Adam#In_Islam" title="Adam">Adam</a>, whom Muslims believe was consoled by Gabriel sometime after <a href="/wiki/Fall_of_man" title="Fall of man">the Fall</a>, too.<sup id="cite_ref-:0_58-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-:0-58"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>56<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> He is known by many names in Islam, such as "keeper of holiness".<sup id="cite_ref-59" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-59"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>57<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> In Hadith traditions, Jibril is said to have six hundred wings.<sup id="cite_ref-Sunnah.com_Muslim_60-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Sunnah.com_Muslim-60"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>58<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>In Islam, the tree of souls is referred to as the <a href="/wiki/Sidrat_al-Muntaha" title="Sidrat al-Muntaha">Sidrat al-Muntaha</a> (and is identified as a <i><a href="/wiki/Ziziphus_spina-christi" title="Ziziphus spina-christi">Ziziphus spina-christi</a></i>).
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="As_a_messenger">As a messenger</h3><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Gabriel&action=edit&section=14" title="Edit section: As a messenger"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<p>Muslims believe that Gabriel was tasked with transmitting the scriptures from God to the <a href="/wiki/Prophets_and_messengers_in_Islam" title="Prophets and messengers in Islam">prophets and messengers</a>, as <a href="/wiki/Asbab_al-Nuzul" title="Asbab al-Nuzul">Asbab al-Nuzul</a> or revelation.<sup id="cite_ref-61" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-61"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>59<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> When Muhammad was questioned which angel is revealing the holy scriptures, he told the Jews they are revealed by Gabriel.<sup id="cite_ref-FOOTNOTENoegelWheeler2002218_62-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTENoegelWheeler2002218-62"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>60<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Muslims also revere Gabriel for several events that predate what they regard as the first revelation narrated in the Quran. Muslims believe that Gabriel was the angel who informed <a href="/wiki/Zechariah_in_Islam" title="Zechariah in Islam">Zechariah</a> of the <a href="/wiki/Nativity_of_John_the_Baptist" title="Nativity of John the Baptist">Nativity of John the Baptist</a>, as well as <a href="/wiki/Mary_in_Islam" title="Mary in Islam">Mary</a> about the future <a href="/wiki/Nativity_of_Jesus" title="Nativity of Jesus">nativity of Jesus</a>;<sup id="cite_ref-63" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-63"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>61<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-64" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-64"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>62<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> and that Gabriel was one of three angels who had earlier informed <a href="/wiki/Abraham_in_Islam" title="Abraham in Islam">Abraham</a> of the birth of <a href="/wiki/Isaac_in_Islam" title="Isaac in Islam">Isaac</a> (<span class="plainlinks"><a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://quran.com/51?startingVerse=24">51:24–30</a></span>).<sup id="cite_ref-65" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-65"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>63<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Gabriel also makes a famous appearance in the <a href="/wiki/Hadith_of_Gabriel" title="Hadith of Gabriel">Hadith of Gabriel</a>, in which he questions Muhammad on the core tenets of Islam.<sup id="cite_ref-EoQ_54-3" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-EoQ-54"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>52<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Gabriel is also believed to have delivered punishment <a href="/wiki/Sodomites_in_Islam" class="mw-redirect" title="Sodomites in Islam">from God to the Sodomites</a> by leveling the entire city of <a href="/wiki/Sodom_and_Gomorrah" title="Sodom and Gomorrah">Sodom</a> with the tip of his wing.<sup id="cite_ref-Nabi-Nabi_Allah:_Kisah_Para_Nabi_dan_Rasul_Allah_dalam_Al-Qur'an_66-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Nabi-Nabi_Allah:_Kisah_Para_Nabi_dan_Rasul_Allah_dalam_Al-Qur'an-66"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>64<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> According to a Hadith narrated by <a href="/wiki/Abu_Dharr_al-Ghifari" title="Abu Dharr al-Ghifari">Abu Dharr al-Ghifari</a>, which is compiled by <a href="/wiki/Al-Hakim_al-Tirmidhi" title="Al-Hakim al-Tirmidhi">al-Hakim al-Tirmidhi</a>, Gabriel has the ability to regulate feeling or perception in humans, particularly happiness or sadness.<sup id="cite_ref-Suyuti;_angel_Jibril_67-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Suyuti;_angel_Jibril-67"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>65<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="As_a_warrior">As a warrior</h3><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Gabriel&action=edit&section=15" title="Edit section: As a warrior"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Muhammad_at_Badr.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4a/Muhammad_at_Badr.jpg/250px-Muhammad_at_Badr.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="358" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4a/Muhammad_at_Badr.jpg/500px-Muhammad_at_Badr.jpg 2x" data-file-width="910" data-file-height="1302"></a><figcaption>Muhammad at the <a href="/wiki/Battle_of_Badr" title="Battle of Badr">Battle of Badr</a>, advised by an angel (<a href="/wiki/Siyer-i_Nebi" title="Siyer-i Nebi">Siyer-i Nebi</a>, 16th century)</figcaption></figure>
<p>Gabriel is believed to have helped Muhammad overcome his adversaries significantly against an <a href="/wiki/Ifrit" title="Ifrit">ifrit</a> during the <a href="/wiki/Isra_and_Mi%27raj" class="mw-redirect" title="Isra and Mi'raj">Night Journey</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-68" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-68"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>66<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-69" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-69"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>67<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Gabriel is also believed to have helped Muhammad overcome his adversaries during the <a href="/wiki/Battle_of_Badr" title="Battle of Badr">Battle of Badr</a>, where according to scholars and clerics of Islam, the various hadiths, both authentics and inauthentics, has mentioned that Gabriel,<sup id="cite_ref-Misri2015_70-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Misri2015-70"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>68<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Michael, <a href="/wiki/Israfil" title="Israfil">Raphael</a>,<sup id="cite_ref-Israfil_71-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Israfil-71"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>69<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-74" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-74"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>N 3<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-76" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-76"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>N 4<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> and thousands of <a href="/wiki/Angels_in_Islam" title="Angels in Islam">best angels</a> from third level of heaven, all came to the battle of Badr by impersonating the appearance of <a href="/wiki/Zubayr_ibn_al-Awwam" title="Zubayr ibn al-Awwam">Zubayr ibn al-Awwam</a>, a <a href="/wiki/Companions_of_the_Prophet" title="Companions of the Prophet">Companion of the Prophet</a> and bodyguard of the prophet.<sup id="cite_ref-78" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-78"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>N 5<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-Tarikh_Dimashq_2_79-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Tarikh_Dimashq_2-79"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>74<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> This is deemed as Zubayr's honor according to Islamic belief.<sup id="cite_ref-FOOTNOTERizqullah2005410_80-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTERizqullah2005410-80"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>75<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-FOOTNOTEAbasoomarAbasoomar2016_81-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAbasoomarAbasoomar2016-81"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>76<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-83" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-83"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>N 6<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Meanwhile, <a href="/wiki/Safiur_Rahman_Mubarakpuri" class="mw-redirect" title="Safiur Rahman Mubarakpuri">Safiur Rahman Mubarakpuri</a> has recorded in his <a href="/wiki/Historiography" title="Historiography">historiography</a> works of Quran and Hadith revelation in <a href="/wiki/Prophetic_biography" class="mw-redirect" title="Prophetic biography">Prophetic biography</a>, that <a href="/wiki/Sa%27d_ibn_Abi_Waqqas" title="Sa'd ibn Abi Waqqas">Sa'd ibn Abi Waqqas</a> testified he saw two unidentified warriors clad in white had protected Muhammad during the <a href="/wiki/Battle_of_Uhud" title="Battle of Uhud">Battle of Uhud</a>, that later being confirmed by Muhammad those two unidentified warriors were Jibril and Michael in disguise.<sup id="cite_ref-eve_of_battle_of_Uhud_&_Ahzab_84-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-eve_of_battle_of_Uhud_&_Ahzab-84"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>78<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Moreover, he is believed to have further encouraged Muhammad to wage war and attack the <a href="/wiki/Jewish_tribes_of_Arabia" title="Jewish tribes of Arabia">Jewish tribe</a> of <a href="/wiki/Banu_Qurayza" title="Banu Qurayza">Banu Qurayza</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-Reynolds2014_55-3" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Reynolds2014-55"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>53<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-Ṣaḥīḥ_Al-Bukhārī_The_Translation_of_the_Meanings_of_Sahih_Al-Bukhari:_Arabic-English_·_Volume_5_85-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Ṣaḥīḥ_Al-Bukhārī_The_Translation_of_the_Meanings_of_Sahih_Al-Bukhari:_Arabic-English_·_Volume_5-85"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>79<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Another appearance of Gabriel in Islamic religious texts were found in numerous Hadiths during the <a href="/wiki/Battle_of_Hunayn" title="Battle of Hunayn">Battle of Hunayn</a>, where the Gabriel stood next to Muhammad.<sup id="cite_ref-The_Book_of_the_Jihad_of_'Ali_Ibn_Tahir_Al-Sulami_(d._1106)_86-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-The_Book_of_the_Jihad_of_'Ali_Ibn_Tahir_Al-Sulami_(d._1106)-86"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>80<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Gabriel is also said to have fought <a href="/wiki/Iblis" title="Iblis">Iblis</a>, when the latter tempted <a href="/wiki/Jesus_in_Islam" title="Jesus in Islam">ʿĪsā</a> (Jesus).<sup id="cite_ref-87" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-87"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>81<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> <a href="/wiki/Ibn_Barrajan" title="Ibn Barrajan">Ibn Barrajan</a> regards Gabriel to be an angel created from fire, like Iblis, thus settling Gabriel symbolically into the head of opposition to the leader of the devils.<sup id="cite_ref-88" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-88"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>82<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Other <a href="/wiki/Islamic_holy_books" title="Islamic holy books">Islamic texts</a> and some <a href="/wiki/Apocryphal_literature" class="mw-redirect" title="Apocryphal literature">apocryphal literature</a> also supported Gabriel's role as a celestial warrior.<sup id="cite_ref-Reynolds2014_55-4" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Reynolds2014-55"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>53<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-89" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-89"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>83<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Though alternate theories exist, whether the occurrence of the <a href="/wiki/Holy_Spirit" title="Holy Spirit">Holy Spirit</a> in the Quran refers to Gabriel or not, remains an issue of scholarly debate.<sup class="noprint Inline-Template Template-Fact" style="white-space:nowrap;">[<i><a href="/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed" title="Wikipedia:Citation needed"><span title="This claim needs references to reliable sources. (February 2022)">citation needed</span></a></i>]</sup> However, a clear distinction between apocryphal and Quranic references to Gabriel is that the former doesn't designate him as the Holy Spirit in the <a href="/wiki/Book_of_Enoch" title="Book of Enoch">First Book of Enoch</a>, which narrates the story of Gabriel defeating the <a href="/wiki/Nephilim" title="Nephilim">Nephilim</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-Reynolds2014_55-5" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Reynolds2014-55"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>53<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="Other_traditions">Other traditions</h2><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Gabriel&action=edit&section=16" title="Edit section: Other traditions"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<p>The <a href="/wiki/Yazidis" title="Yazidis">Yazidis</a> worship <a href="/wiki/Seven_Archangels" title="Seven Archangels">Seven Archangels</a>, including Jabra'il (Gabriel), Mikha'il (Michael), Rapha'il (Raphael), <a href="/w/index.php?title=Dedra%27il&action=edit&redlink=1" class="new" title="Dedra'il (page does not exist)">Dedra'il</a>, <a href="/wiki/Azrael" title="Azrael">Azra'il</a>, <a href="/w/index.php?title=Shamka%27il&action=edit&redlink=1" class="new" title="Shamka'il (page does not exist)">Shamka'il</a>, and <a href="/wiki/Azazil" title="Azazil">Azazil</a>, who are <a href="/wiki/Emanationism" title="Emanationism">emanations from God</a> with which God entrusted the world. Other angels in <a href="/wiki/Yazidism" title="Yazidism">Yazidism</a> include <a href="/wiki/Israfil" title="Israfil">Azrafil</a>, Nekir and Nukir.<sup id="cite_ref-Empson1928_90-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Empson1928-90"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>84<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> The Yazidis associate Gabriel with <a href="/wiki/Taw%C3%BBs%C3%AE_Melek" title="Tawûsî Melek">Tawûsî Melek</a> (the "Peacock Angel").<sup id="cite_ref-Asatrian2003_91-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Asatrian2003-91"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>85<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p><a href="/wiki/Yazd%C3%A2nism" title="Yazdânism">Yazdânism</a> and <a href="/wiki/Yarsanism" title="Yarsanism">Yarsanism</a> share many elements with Yazidism, including seven secondary divine manifestations, emanationism and the <a href="/wiki/Incarnation" title="Incarnation">incarnation</a> of the archangel Gabriel (<i>Pir Benjamin</i> in Yarsanism).
</p><p><a href="/wiki/Mandaeism" title="Mandaeism">Mandaeans</a> venerate <a href="/wiki/Ptahil" title="Ptahil">Ptahil</a> as the "Fourth Life" (the third of three emanations from the First Life). Ptahil is an <i><a href="/wiki/Uthra" title="Uthra">uthra</a></i>, identified with Gabriel, who creates the poorly made material world with the help of <a href="/wiki/Ruha" title="Ruha">Ruha</a>, a sinful and fallen female ruler who inhabits the <a href="/wiki/World_of_Darkness_(Mandaeism)" title="World of Darkness (Mandaeism)">World of Darkness</a>. Ruha and Ptahil's roles in creation vary, with each gaining control when the other's power subsides.<sup id="cite_ref-Buckley1982_92-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Buckley1982-92"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>86<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> According to <a href="/wiki/Brikha_Nasoraia" title="Brikha Nasoraia">Brikha Nasoraia</a>, the creation of <a href="/wiki/Tibil" title="Tibil">the material world</a> occurs by <a href="/wiki/Hayyi_Rabbi" title="Hayyi Rabbi">God's</a> command, but is delegated to Ptahil (a subservient emanation or <i>uthra</i>) with the assistance of Gabriel and others.<sup id="cite_ref-Nasoraia2012_93-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Nasoraia2012-93"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>87<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="Art,_entertainment,_and_media"><span id="Art.2C_entertainment.2C_and_media"></span>Art, entertainment, and media</h2><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Gabriel&action=edit&section=17" title="Edit section: Art, entertainment, and media"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<p>Angels are described as pure spirits.<sup id="cite_ref-gorgievski_94-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-gorgievski-94"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>88<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-longhurst_95-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-longhurst-95"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>89<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> The lack of a defined form allows artists wide latitude in depicting them.<sup id="cite_ref-96" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-96"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>90<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Amelia R. Brown draws comparisons in Byzantine iconography between portrayals of angels and the conventions used to depict court eunuchs. Mainly from the Caucasus, they tended to have light eyes, hair, and skin; and those "castrated in childhood developed a distinctive skeletal structure, lacked full masculine musculature, body hair and beards ..." As officials, they would wear a white tunic decorated with gold. Brown suggests that "Byzantine artists drew, consciously or not, on this iconography of the court eunuch".<sup id="cite_ref-brown_97-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-brown-97"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>91<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Some recent popular works on angels consider Gabriel to be female or <a href="/wiki/Androgynous" class="mw-redirect" title="Androgynous">androgynous</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-Giovetti1993_98-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Giovetti1993-98"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>92<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-Godwin1990_99-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Godwin1990-99"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>93<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Painting_and_sculpture">Painting and sculpture</h3><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Gabriel&action=edit&section=18" title="Edit section: Painting and sculpture"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<p>Gabriel is most often portrayed in the context of the <a href="/wiki/Annunciation" title="Annunciation">Annunciation</a>. In 2008, a 16th-century drawing by Lucas van Leyden of the Netherlands was discovered. George R. Goldner, chairman of the department of prints and drawings at New York's <a href="/wiki/Metropolitan_Museum_of_Art" title="Metropolitan Museum of Art">Metropolitan Museum of Art</a>, suggested that the sketch was for a stained glass window. "The fact that the archangel is an ordinary-looking person and not an idealized boy is typical of the artist", said Goldner.<sup id="cite_ref-100" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-100"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>94<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>The Military Order of Saint Gabriel was established to recognize "individuals who have made significant contributions to the U.S. Army Public Affairs community and practice". The medallion depicts St. Gabriel sounding a trumpet, while the obverse displays the Army Public Affairs emblem.<sup id="cite_ref-101" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-101"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>95<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>

<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Festivals">Festivals</h3><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Gabriel&action=edit&section=19" title="Edit section: Festivals"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<p>Baltimore's (Maryland) "<a href="/wiki/Little_Italy,_Baltimore" title="Little Italy, Baltimore">Little Italy</a>" neighborhood has for over 80 years hosted an annual "end of summer" St. Gabriel Festival that features a procession with a statue of the saint carried through the streets.<sup id="cite_ref-102" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-102"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>96<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-103" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-103"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>97<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Video_games">Video games</h3><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Gabriel&action=edit&section=20" title="Edit section: Video games"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<p>The first-person shooter game <i><a href="/wiki/Ultrakill" title="Ultrakill">Ultrakill</a></i> features Gabriel <a href="/wiki/Ultrakill#Plot" title="Ultrakill">as the main antagonist</a> and narrative protagonist, although Gabriel in game and Gabriel in Biblical canon share many differences.
</p>
